events.text: Patient is a 58-year-old Asian male with a history of well-controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension, presenting with a new irregular pigmented macule on his mid-back.

At age 58, patient was diagnosed with superficial spreading melanoma (ICD-O morphology 8743/3) of the skin of the trunk (ICD-O topography C44.5), staged as Stage IV (T3aN2aM1c) with synchronous liver metastases discovered on staging workup.

At age 58, excisional biopsy of the mid-back lesion revealed superficial spreading melanoma with Breslow thickness of 2.2 mm, Clark level IV, without ulceration, mitotic rate 5/mm2, no microsatellites, no lymphovascular invasion, and negative margins.
At age 58, wide local excision with 2 cm margins of the mid-back melanoma was performed with primary closure.
At age 58, sentinel lymph node biopsy of the bilateral axillae showed 2 of 5 total sentinel nodes positive for metastatic melanoma (1 right axillary, 1 left axillary), largest deposit 2.8 mm.
At age 58, sentinel node pathology confirmed metastatic melanoma in 2 of 5 nodes from bilateral axillae, no extracapsular extension, S100 and SOX10 immunohistochemistry positive.

At age 58, staging CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis revealed two hepatic lesions measuring 2.5 cm and 1.8 cm in the right hepatic lobe, suspicious for metastatic disease, with no pulmonary or osseous metastases.
At age 58, MRI of the abdomen confirmed two hepatic metastases in segments VI and VII of the right liver lobe, with peripheral enhancement and washout pattern.
At age 58, PET/CT showed FDG-avid hepatic lesions (SUVmax 8.5 and 6.2) and mild bilateral axillary FDG uptake consistent with known nodal metastases.
At age 58, MRI brain with gadolinium showed a small 0.7 cm enhancing focus in the left temporal lobe, indeterminate but suspicious for early brain metastasis.

At age 58, comprehensive genomic profiling of the primary tumor identified BRAF V600E mutation (allele frequency 44%), NRAS wild-type, TERT promoter mutation (C228T), PTEN deletion, MAP2K1 amplification, and tumor mutational burden of 15 mutations per megabase with PD-L1 combined positive score of 22.

At age 58, serum LDH was elevated at 356 U/L, and the case was discussed at multidisciplinary tumor board where consensus was to proceed with combined immunotherapy given brain metastasis (M1d staging) and high PD-L1 expression.
At age 58, stereotactic radiosurgery was delivered to the left temporal brain lesion at 24 Gy in 1 fraction.
At age 58, combination ipilimumab 3 mg/kg plus nivolumab 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks was initiated for 4 cycles for metastatic BRAF V600E melanoma with brain and hepatic involvement.
At age 58, patient developed grade 2 immune-related diarrhea after cycle 2 and grade 1 transaminase elevation (ALT 95 U/L), managed with loperamide and close monitoring without treatment delay.

At age 58, blood glucose became more difficult to control with hemoglobin A1c rising from 6.8% to 8.2%, attributed to potential immune-mediated effect on pancreatic function and corticosteroid use; insulin was added to his diabetic regimen.
At age 58, after completing 4 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab induction, maintenance nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks was started.
At age 58, restaging CT at 12 weeks showed partial response with 30% reduction in hepatic metastases per RECIST 1.1.
At age 59, MRI brain at 3 months post-SRS showed decreased enhancement of the left temporal lesion to 0.3 cm with no new intracranial lesions.
At age 59, LDH trended down to 268 U/L reflecting partial response to immunotherapy and patient maintained ECOG performance status of 1.
At age 59, 6-month restaging PET/CT showed continued decrease in hepatic FDG uptake with partial metabolic response.
At age 59, 9-month restaging CT showed stable hepatic lesions without further reduction and no new metastatic disease.

At age 59, after 12 months of nivolumab maintenance with stable disease (best response partial response), LDH was 232 U/L and the patient was continued on nivolumab with plan to reassess at 18 months.
At age 59, 12-month MRI brain showed complete resolution of the left temporal lesion with no new intracranial disease.

At age 60, at 18 months of nivolumab maintenance, restaging showed continued stable hepatic disease with LDH 225 U/L, and the decision was made to continue nivolumab given sustained disease control and maintained quality of life with BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy held in reserve for future progression.

scenario_blurb: Stage III/IV melanoma with BRAF V600E mutation. Treated with combination BRAF/MEK inhibitors (dabrafenib/trametinib) and/or checkpoint immunotherapy (nivolumab plus ipilimumab, pembrolizumab). Some with brain metastases. Include LDH levels.
